facts.text: Superman first appeared in Action Comics #1, published on April 18, 1938, with a cover price of 10 cents.
Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster created Superman while they were teenagers living in Cleveland, Ohio.
Action Comics #1 is the most valuable comic book in history, with a near-mint copy selling for over $6 million at auction.
Superman was the first comic book superhero, establishing the entire superhero genre that followed.
"Look, up in the sky! It's a bird! It's a plane! It's Superman!" — this iconic introduction debuted on the Superman radio show in 1940 and became permanently embedded in pop culture.
Superman's birth name is Kal-El, which Jerry Siegel intended to echo the Hebrew word for 'voice of God.'
Krypton, Superman's home planet, was destroyed by internal geological instability, forcing Jor-El to send his infant son to Earth.
Jor-El and Lara Lor-Van are Superman's biological parents on Krypton.
Jonathan and Martha Kent found baby Kal-El's rocket in a field near Smallville, Kansas, and raised him as Clark Kent.
"You will travel far, my little Kal-El. But we will never leave you — even in the face of our death." — Jor-El's farewell to his son as he sends him to Earth.
Superman's powers come from Earth's yellow sun, which energizes his Kryptonian cells in ways that Krypton's red sun never could.
Kryptonite, the radioactive remains of Krypton, is Superman's most famous weakness, capable of weakening and potentially killing him.
Green Kryptonite is the most common variety and is lethal to Kryptonians with prolonged exposure.
Red Kryptonite has unpredictable effects on Superman, ranging from personality changes to physical transformations.
Gold Kryptonite can permanently strip a Kryptonian of their powers under certain continuities.
Blue Kryptonite is harmless to Superman but deadly to Bizarro, Superman's imperfect duplicate.
Superman's core powers include super strength, flight, invulnerability, super speed, heat vision, freeze breath, X-ray vision, and super hearing.
Originally, Superman could not fly — he could only leap great distances. The phrase 'able to leap tall buildings in a single bound' comes from this era.
Flight was added to Superman's abilities for the Fleischer Studios animated cartoons in 1941 because animating leaping looked awkward.
"It's not an 'S.' On my world, it means hope." — Superman explaining the meaning of his chest symbol in Man of Steel (2013).
The S-shield on Superman's chest has been reinterpreted multiple times, from a simple letter to a Kryptonian family crest meaning 'hope.'
Clark Kent works as a reporter at the Daily Planet, Metropolis's largest newspaper.
Perry White is the editor-in-chief of the Daily Planet, known for his catchphrase 'Great Caesar's ghost!'
Jimmy Olsen is a young photojournalist at the Daily Planet and one of Superman's closest allies, often called 'Superman's pal.'
Lois Lane first appeared alongside Superman in Action Comics #1 and has been his primary love interest ever since.
Lois Lane was partially inspired by real-life reporter Nellie Bly and actress Glenda Farrell's portrayal of reporter Torchy Blane.
"I'm here to fight for truth, and justice, and the American way." — Superman's mission statement as told to Lois Lane in Superman (1978).
Metropolis, Superman's home city, is often considered a fictional stand-in for New York City, specifically representing its optimistic daytime persona.
There is a real town called Metropolis in Illinois that has a Superman statue in the town square and officially declared itself the 'Hometown of Superman.'
Lex Luthor is Superman's greatest enemy, a brilliant billionaire industrialist who views Superman as a threat to human achievement.
Lex Luthor was originally depicted as a mad scientist with red hair before being redesigned as a bald corporate magnate in the 1980s.
"Do you know the oldest lie in America, Senator? It's that power can be innocent." — Lex Luthor in Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016).
General Zod is a Kryptonian military leader who was banished to the Phantom Zone for attempting a coup on Krypton.
"Kneel before Zod!" — General Zod's iconic command in Superman II (1980), delivered by Terence Stamp, became one of cinema's most quoted villain lines.
Brainiac is an alien android who collects and miniaturizes cities from various planets, including Kandor from Krypton.
Kandor, the capital city of Krypton, was shrunk and bottled by Brainiac before the planet's destruction.
Doomsday is the monster that killed Superman in the famous 1992 comic storyline 'The Death of Superman.'
The Death of Superman storyline in 1992-1993 was a massive media event, making national news and selling millions of copies.
Superman #75, featuring Superman's death, was published on November 18, 1992, and came in a sealed black polybag with a memorial armband.
After Superman's death, four replacement Supermen appeared: Steel, Superboy, the Eradicator, and Cyborg Superman.
Superman eventually returned from the dead, having been placed in a Kryptonian regeneration matrix in the Fortress of Solitude.
The Fortress of Solitude is Superman's Arctic headquarters, typically depicted as a crystalline structure containing Kryptonian technology.
Darkseid, the tyrannical ruler of the planet Apokolips, is one of Superman's most powerful adversaries and seeks the Anti-Life Equation.
Bizarro is an imperfect clone of Superman who does everything in reverse, living on the cube-shaped Bizarro World.
"Me am not Superman! Me am Bizarro!" — Bizarro's backwards speech pattern has been a staple of his character since his creation.
Parasite is a villain who can absorb Superman's powers and life force through physical contact.
Metallo is a cyborg villain with a Kryptonite heart, making him a walking weakness for Superman.
Mr. Mxyzptlk is a fifth-dimensional imp who torments Superman with reality-warping powers and can only be sent home if tricked into saying his name backwards.
Superman is a founding member of the Justice League of America, typically serving as the team's moral compass.
The Justice League was first introduced in The Brave and the Bold #28 in 1960, with Superman as a founding member.
Superman and Batman are often referred to as the 'World's Finest' duo, a title from their long-running team-up comic.
"The last time you inspired anyone was when you were dead." — Batman to Superman in Hush (2002), highlighting their complex relationship.
Superman's relationship with Batman represents opposing approaches to heroism — hope versus fear, light versus darkness.
Wonder Woman, Superman, and Batman form DC Comics' 'Trinity,' the three most iconic heroes in the DC Universe.
Supergirl, also known as Kara Zor-El, is Superman's cousin who also survived Krypton's destruction.
Superboy, in various incarnations, has been either a young Clark Kent or a clone combining Superman and Lex Luthor's DNA.
Krypto the Superdog is Superman's pet dog from Krypton, possessing all the same powers as his master.
The Phantom Zone is an interdimensional prison used by Kryptonians to exile dangerous criminals.
Superman's vulnerability to magic is one of his lesser-known weaknesses — magical attacks affect him just like anyone else.
"What's special about Superman is that he will always make the right choice." — Christopher Reeve on why Superman endures as a character.
The original Superman comic strip was rejected by multiple newspaper syndicates before being picked up by DC Comics (then National Allied Publications).
Jerry Siegel wrote the first Superman story during a sleepless night in 1934, with Joe Shuster illustrating it the next morning.
Siegel and Shuster sold the rights to Superman to DC Comics for just $130 in 1938, a decision they fought to reverse for decades.
The legal battle between Siegel, Shuster, and DC Comics over Superman's ownership lasted from the 1940s through the 2010s.
In 1975, Warner Communications agreed to give Siegel and Shuster annual stipends and credit on all Superman productions after a public campaign.
Joe Shuster's original design for Superman was influenced by circus strongmen and the actor Douglas Fairbanks Sr.
Clark Kent's mild-mannered persona and glasses disguise was inspired by Harold Lloyd, the bespectacled silent film star.
Superman's cape and costume were inspired by the costumes of circus acrobats and strongmen of the 1930s.
The Superman radio serial ran from 1940 to 1951 and introduced many elements later adopted by the comics, including Kryptonite and Jimmy Olsen.
Kryptonite was invented for the Superman radio show in 1943 to give the voice actor, Bud Collyer, time off — a substitute could groan weakly instead of performing.
The Superman radio show took a progressive stand against the Ku Klux Klan in 1946, exposing their rituals and code words to millions of listeners.
Kirk Alyn became the first actor to play Superman on screen in the 1948 serial film Superman.
George Reeves starred as Superman in Adventures of Superman, the classic TV series that ran from 1952 to 1958.
George Reeves' portrayal was so beloved that children would reportedly bring loaded guns to his appearances, wanting to see if bullets really bounced off him.
The mysterious death of George Reeves in 1959, officially ruled a suicide, has been the subject of much speculation and was explored in the 2006 film Hollywoodland.
"You'll believe a man can fly" — the tagline for Richard Donner's 1978 Superman film promised something revolutionary and delivered on it completely.
Christopher Reeve was a relative unknown when he was cast as Superman in 1978, beating out hundreds of candidates including Robert Redford, Burt Reynolds, and Sylvester Stallone.
Marlon Brando was paid $3.7 million for just 12 days of work as Jor-El in Superman (1978), plus a percentage of the gross.

"They can be a great people, Kal-El; they wish to be. They only lack the light to show the way. For this reason above all — their capacity for good — I have sent them you, my only son." — Jor-El's speech to Kal-El in Superman (1978).
John Williams composed the iconic Superman march for the 1978 film, creating one of the most recognizable themes in movie history.
Richard Donner directed Superman (1978) and most of Superman II before being replaced by Richard Lester due to conflicts with producers Alexander and Ilya Salkind.
The 'Donner Cut' of Superman II was finally completed and released in 2006, using Donner's original footage and vision.
Gene Hackman played Lex Luthor in Superman (1978) and Superman II (1980), bringing sardonic humor to the role.
"Some people can read War and Peace and come away thinking it's a simple adventure story." — Lex Luthor showing his intellectual arrogance in Superman (1978).
Margot Kidder's portrayal of Lois Lane in the Christopher Reeve films redefined the character as tough, witty, and independent.
The flying sequences in Superman (1978) were achieved through a combination of front projection, wire work, and optical effects that revolutionized visual effects.
Superman (1978) was nominated for three Academy Awards and won a Special Achievement Award for visual effects.
Christopher Reeve was praised for creating two distinctly different performances — the confident Superman and the bumbling Clark Kent — using only body language and voice.
"Is it really you? I never thought — I mean, I always hoped, but —" — Lois Lane's emotional reunion monologue captures the audience's own disbelief at Superman's return.
Terence Stamp's portrayal of General Zod in Superman II (1980) was so iconic that the character was largely defined by his performance for decades.
Superman III (1983) starred Richard Pryor as a comedic computer genius, shifting the franchise toward comedy over drama.
Superman III features a memorable sequence where Superman is split into an evil Superman and a good Clark Kent who must fight each other.
Superman IV: The Quest for Peace (1987) dealt with nuclear disarmament but suffered from severe budget cuts by the Cannon Group.
Christopher Reeve was deeply committed to the anti-nuclear message of Superman IV and co-wrote the story.
Christopher Reeve was paralyzed in a horse-riding accident in 1995 and became a tireless advocate for spinal cord injury research until his death in 2004.
Christopher Reeve's courage and activism after his injury led many to call him a real-life Superman, embodying the character's values of perseverance and hope.
"A hero is an ordinary individual who finds the strength to persevere and endure in spite of overwhelming obstacles." — Christopher Reeve.
Superman Returns (2006), directed by Bryan Singer, served as a sequel to the first two Christopher Reeve films, ignoring III and IV.
Brandon Routh starred in Superman Returns and deliberately channeled Christopher Reeve's performance as both Superman and Clark Kent.
Superman Returns featured Marlon Brando's likeness and voice as Jor-El using archival footage from the 1978 film.
Kevin Spacey played Lex Luthor in Superman Returns, bringing a more menacing interpretation to the role.
Superman Returns grossed $391 million worldwide but was considered underperforming given its $204 million budget.
Man of Steel (2013), directed by Zack Snyder, rebooted the Superman franchise with a grittier, more realistic tone.
Henry Cavill was cast as Superman in Man of Steel, becoming the first non-American actor to play the character in a major film.
Henry Cavill had previously auditioned for Superman Returns and was even considered for Superman as early as 2004.
"You're not just anyone. One day, you're going to have to make a choice. You have to decide what kind of man you want to grow up to be." — Jonathan Kent to young Clark in Man of Steel.
Michael Shannon's portrayal of General Zod in Man of Steel presented the villain as a sympathetic figure whose only purpose was to protect Krypton.
Russell Crowe played Jor-El in Man of Steel, giving the character a more action-oriented portrayal than Marlon Brando's version.
Amy Adams played Lois Lane in Man of Steel and its sequels, portraying her as a Pulitzer Prize-winning investigative journalist.
Man of Steel's controversial climax, where Superman kills Zod by snapping his neck, sparked intense debate among fans about Superman's no-kill rule.
"If you seek his monument, look around you." — the epitaph on Jonathan Kent's gravestone in Man of Steel, adapted from Christopher Wren's epitaph.
Hans Zimmer composed the score for Man of Steel, deliberately creating a new musical identity rather than using John Williams' iconic theme.
Man of Steel grossed $668 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing Superman film at the time.
Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016) brought Superman and Batman together on screen for the first time in a live-action film.
Ben Affleck co-starred as Batman in Batman v Superman, with the conflict driven by Batman's fear of Superman's unchecked power.
The 'Martha' scene in Batman v Superman — where Batman stops fighting upon learning their mothers share the name — became one of the most debated moments in superhero cinema.
Jesse Eisenberg's unconventional portrayal of Lex Luthor in Batman v Superman as a manic tech mogul divided audiences and critics.
Gal Gadot made her debut as Wonder Woman in Batman v Superman, joining Superman and Batman in the climactic battle against Doomsday.
Superman dies at the end of Batman v Superman fighting Doomsday, paralleling the Death of Superman comic storyline.
Batman v Superman grossed $873 million worldwide despite polarized critical reception.
Justice League (2017) featured Superman's resurrection, with the hero initially confused and hostile before regaining his memories.
Zack Snyder's Justice League (2021), the four-hour director's cut, restored Snyder's original vision and featured significantly more Superman content.
"Not impressed" — Superman's deadpan response to Steppenwolf in Zack Snyder's Justice League, demonstrating his overwhelming power.
Henry Cavill's return as Superman was teased in a Black Adam (2022) post-credits scene, though the DCEU was subsequently rebooted.
The 2025 Superman film, directed by James Gunn, launched the new DC Universe with David Corenswet as Superman and Rachel Brosnahan as Lois Lane.
James Gunn's Superman (2025) aimed to present a more optimistic and hopeful version of the character after the darker DCEU interpretation.
Nicholas Hoult was cast as Lex Luthor in James Gunn's Superman (2025).
Lois & Clark: The New Adventures of Superman (1993-1997) starred Dean Cain and Teri Hatcher and focused on the romantic relationship between Clark and Lois.
Teri Hatcher's portrayal of Lois Lane in Lois & Clark was praised for being smart, ambitious, and a true equal to Superman.
Smallville (2001-2011) ran for ten seasons on The WB/CW, following Clark Kent's journey from teenager to Superman.
Tom Welling starred as Clark Kent in Smallville, with the show's famous rule being 'no tights, no flights' — Clark didn't fully become Superman until the series finale.
Smallville introduced many elements that influenced later Superman adaptations, including the meteor freak-of-the-week format and Clark's complex relationship with a young Lex Luthor.
Michael Rosenbaum's portrayal of Lex Luthor in Smallville is considered one of the definitive versions of the character.
"I don't want to be anybody's hero." — Clark Kent in early Smallville episodes, resisting his destiny before eventually embracing it.
Smallville became the longest-running North American sci-fi series at the time, surpassing Stargate SG-1.
Superman & Lois (2021-2024) starred Tyler Hoechlin and Elizabeth Tulloch, exploring Superman as a father of two teenage sons.
Superman & Lois was praised for its emotional depth and for presenting Superman dealing with relatable family challenges alongside cosmic threats.
The Fleischer Superman cartoons (1941-1943) are considered masterpieces of animation and were among the most expensive cartoons produced at the time.
The Fleischer cartoons established many visual elements of Superman, including his method of flying and the look of Metropolis.
Superman: The Animated Series (1996-2000) was produced by Bruce Timm and Paul Dini and existed within the DC Animated Universe.
Tim Daly voiced Superman in Superman: The Animated Series, while Dana Delany voiced Lois Lane and Clancy Brown voiced Lex Luthor.
Clancy Brown's voice performance as Lex Luthor in the animated series is widely regarded as the definitive animated portrayal of the character.
The DC Animated Universe's version of Superman crossed over with Batman: The Animated Series and later continued in Justice League and Justice League Unlimited.

"I feel like I live in a world made of cardboard, always taking constant care not to break something, to break someone." — Superman in Justice League Unlimited, releasing his full power against Darkseid.
All-Star Superman (2005-2008) by Grant Morrison and Frank Quitely is considered one of the greatest Superman stories ever told.
In All-Star Superman, the Man of Steel discovers he is dying from solar radiation overexposure and sets out to complete twelve labors.
"You're much stronger than you think you are. Trust me." — Superman to a suicidal girl in All-Star Superman, one of the most emotional moments in all of comics.
Kingdom Come (1996) by Mark Waid and Alex Ross depicts a dystopian future where an older Superman comes out of retirement to restore order.
Alex Ross's hyper-realistic painted artwork in Kingdom Come is considered some of the finest art in comic book history.
Superman: Red Son (2003) by Mark Millar reimagines Superman's origin with his rocket landing in the Soviet Union instead of Kansas.
In Red Son, Superman becomes the champion of communism rather than the American way, exploring how environment shapes identity.
"Why don't you just put the whole world in a bottle, Superman?" — Lex Luthor's cutting critique in Red Son, questioning Superman's brand of 'protection.'
Superman: Birthright (2003-2004) by Mark Waid modernized Superman's origin and influenced the Man of Steel film.
Superman: Secret Identity (2004) by Kurt Busiek tells the story of a boy named Clark Kent in the real world who develops Superman's powers.
Whatever Happened to the Man of Tomorrow? (1986) by Alan Moore served as the final story of the pre-Crisis Superman, and is considered a perfect farewell.
"This is an imaginary story. Aren't they all?" — Alan Moore's iconic opening line for Whatever Happened to the Man of Tomorrow?
Superman: For All Seasons (1998) by Jeph Loeb and Tim Sale is a coming-of-age tale examining Superman's early years through the four seasons.
The Man of Steel (1986) miniseries by John Byrne rebooted Superman's origin for the post-Crisis DC Universe, making him the sole survivor of Krypton.
John Byrne's reboot eliminated Superboy from continuity, made Lex Luthor a corporate villain, and reduced Superman's power levels.
Superman: Secret Origin (2009-2010) by Geoff Johns became the definitive origin for the post-Infinite Crisis era.
Superman: Up, Up and Away! (2006) told the story of a depowered Clark Kent who must reclaim his abilities and identity.
The Reign of the Supermen storyline following Superman's death introduced Steel (John Henry Irons), who became a hero in his own right.
John Henry Irons, aka Steel, was a weapons engineer who built a suit of armor to honor Superman's legacy after his death.
Shaquille O'Neal starred as Steel in a widely panned 1997 film adaptation that removed all connections to Superman.
Superboy-Prime is an alternate version of Superman from Earth-Prime who becomes one of the most dangerous villains in DC Comics.
The Phantom Zone was created by Jerry Siegel and first appeared in Adventure Comics #283 in 1961.
Superman's power level has varied dramatically throughout his history — in the Silver Age, he could move planets and travel through time.
In the Silver Age, Superman had bizarre powers including super-ventriloquism, super-weaving, and super-mathematics.
Superman once arm-wrestled the entire Justice League simultaneously in a Silver Age comic and won.
Crisis on Infinite Earths (1985) killed the Earth-Two Superman, one of the most significant character deaths in DC history.
The Death of Superman storyline was partly conceived because the writers felt they had exhausted story ideas and wanted to shock the industry.
"Doomsday is coming" graffiti appeared in DC Comics for months before the actual Death of Superman storyline began.
Superman #75 used a single-panel-per-page format for the final battle with Doomsday, creating an increasingly intense rhythm.
The Return of Superman storyline revealed that the Eradicator had placed Superman's body in a Kryptonian regeneration chamber.
Cyborg Superman (Hank Henshaw) impersonated Superman during the Reign of the Supermen and destroyed Coast City, setting up major Green Lantern storylines.
Our Worlds at War (2001) was a massive crossover where Superman fought Imperiex and saw the apparent death of several allies.
Superman has been a member of multiple teams including the Justice League, the Justice Society, and the Legion of Super-Heroes.
The Legion of Super-Heroes, a team of teenage heroes from the 31st century, was inspired by Superman's legend and frequently teamed up with Superboy.
"Truth, Justice, and the American Way" has been Superman's motto since the 1940s radio show, though modern adaptations have updated it.
In 2021, DC updated Superman's motto to 'Truth, Justice, and a Better Tomorrow' for contemporary relevance.
"There is a superhero in all of us, we just need the courage to put on the cape." — This sentiment captures the core of Superman's inspirational mission.
Superman's heat vision was not part of his original power set — it was added in the 1960s, replacing the earlier 'heat of his X-ray vision.'
Superman can survive in space without oxygen, though some modern interpretations require him to hold his breath.
Superman once flew so fast he broke the time barrier and traveled to the future, a power used frequently in Silver Age stories.
Mon-El (Lar Gand) is a Daxamite ally of Superman who has similar powers but is vulnerable to lead instead of Kryptonite.
Power Girl is the Earth-Two version of Supergirl and Superman's cousin from a parallel universe.
The Eradicator is a Kryptonian artifact turned sentient being that has alternated between villain and antihero in Superman stories.
Superman has fought Muhammad Ali in a special 1978 DC Comics publication, Superman vs. Muhammad Ali, with Ali winning a depowered boxing match.
Mongul is a tyrannical alien warlord who has challenged Superman repeatedly, most notably in the classic story 'For the Man Who Has Everything.'
"For the Man Who Has Everything" (1985) by Alan Moore shows Superman trapped in a dream of Krypton never exploding — and he must willingly give it up.
"Burn." — Superman's single devastating word when he finally breaks free and attacks Mongul in For the Man Who Has Everything.
Toyman is one of Superman's rogues who uses deadly toy-themed weapons and has been depicted in various incarnations from campy to terrifying.
Intergang is a Metropolis crime syndicate that uses Apokoliptian technology provided by Darkseid.
Manchester Black is a villain who challenged Superman's moral code in the story 'What's So Funny About Truth, Justice & the American Way?'
"What's So Funny About Truth, Justice & the American Way?" (Action Comics #775, 2001) is considered one of the greatest single-issue Superman stories.
"I won't let you or the Elites take that away from the people who still believe in it." — Superman defending his principles against the Elite's violent methods.
Livewire is an electricity-powered villain who first appeared in Superman: The Animated Series before being introduced into comics.
Hank Henshaw, the Cyborg Superman, is a tragic figure similar to Marvel's Reed Richards who went insane and blamed Superman for his transformation.
Superman's power of super-hearing allows him to hear sounds across the globe, and this ability is often shown as both a gift and a burden.
In some continuities, Superman can see across the entire electromagnetic spectrum, from radio waves to gamma rays.
Superman's freeze breath can lower temperatures to absolute zero, allowing him to instantly freeze objects and foes.
The concept of Superman maintaining a secret identity explored the immigrant experience — hiding one's true self to fit into a new society.
Many scholars have noted that Superman's origin story parallels the story of Moses — a child sent away from destruction by his parents to be raised by others.
Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster were both Jewish, and Superman's story has been widely analyzed as a Jewish immigrant narrative.
Superman was used in World War II propaganda, with covers showing him fighting Axis powers and encouraging war bond purchases.
"I'm here to stand up for people who can't stand up for themselves." — Superman, encapsulating his defining purpose.
Superman has been adapted into every medium: comic books, newspaper strips, radio, film, television, animation, novels, and video games.
The first Superman video game was released for Atari 2600 in 1979.
Superman 64 (1999) for the Nintendo 64 is widely considered one of the worst video games ever made, infamous for its ring-flying missions.
The Injustice: Gods Among Us video game (2013) and comic series explored an alternate universe where Superman becomes a dictator after the Joker tricks him into killing Lois Lane.
In the Injustice storyline, Superman kills the Joker and establishes a totalitarian regime, with Batman leading the resistance.
"I used to believe everyone deserved a second chance. But some aren't looking for a second chance. They're looking for a second victim." — Injustice Superman justifying his authoritarian rule.
Superman has a well-known weakness to magic — spells and magical weapons affect him as if he had no special defenses.
Red sun radiation can strip Superman of his powers, as Kryptonians only gain abilities under a yellow sun.
Superman can sundip — fly directly into the sun — to supercharge his abilities to extreme levels.
Superman Prime One Million, from the DC One Million storyline, is a version of Superman who sundipped for 15,000 years and became godlike.
The Bottle City of Kandor contains the last surviving city of Krypton, shrunk by Brainiac before the planet's destruction.
Superman has grappled with loneliness as the last survivor of an entire civilization throughout his history.
"Even though you've been raised as a human being, you're not one of them." — Jor-El to Superman, highlighting his alien nature.
Superman and Lois Lane were married in the comics in Superman: The Wedding Album (1996), timed to coincide with the Lois & Clark TV show's wedding.
Jon Kent, the son of Superman and Lois Lane, was introduced in 2015 and has carried on his father's legacy as a new Superman.
In 2021, Jon Kent came out as bisexual in Superman: Son of Kal-El #5, making major cultural headlines.
Kon-El (Conner Kent), another Superboy, is a clone created from both Superman and Lex Luthor's DNA.
Superman's Pal Jimmy Olsen had his own solo comic series that ran from 1954 to 1974, featuring numerous bizarre transformations.
Jimmy Olsen's signal watch emits a high-frequency sound that only Superman can hear, allowing him to call for help.
Superman has a no-kill rule that he adheres to in most continuities, believing in the justice system and the value of every life.
"Every person can be a force for good, free to forge his own destiny." — Superman expressing his faith in humanity.
The Superman Curse is an urban legend suggesting that actors who play Superman suffer misfortune, citing George Reeves' death and Christopher Reeve's accident.
Nicolas Cage was set to star in Tim Burton's Superman Lives in the late 1990s, but the project was canceled during pre-production.
The documentary The Death of 'Superman Lives': What Happened? (2015) explored the failed Tim Burton project, including Cage's costume tests.
Kevin Smith wrote a screenplay for Superman Lives, famously recounting producer Jon Peters' bizarre demand to include a giant spider.
J.J. Abrams wrote a Superman screenplay called Superman: Flyby that was nearly produced with various directors attached before being shelved.
Superman's appearance — the spit curl, the red cape, the blue suit — has become the universal symbol for superheroes.
The iconic image of Superman ripping open his shirt to reveal the S-shield is one of the most recognized images in popular culture.
Superman's costume traditionally includes red trunks over blue tights, though modern designs have frequently removed them.
"What I do, I do for the people of this planet." — Superman, stating his commitment to Earth despite his alien origin.
Max Fleischer's Superman cartoons were so expensive to produce that each episode cost approximately $100,000 — equivalent to over $2 million today.
The Fleischer Superman cartoons inspired the look and atmosphere of Batman: The Animated Series decades later.
Superman has appeared on the cover of Time magazine and is considered one of the most recognizable fictional characters in history.
A study found that Superman was one of the top three most recognized fictional characters worldwide, alongside Mickey Mouse and Santa Claus.
Superman: The Movie (1978) was one of the first major superhero blockbusters and paved the way for the modern superhero film genre.
Before Christopher Reeve, the studio considered casting Muhammad Ali, Neil Diamond, and even Dustin Hoffman as Superman.
Christopher Reeve gained 30 pounds of muscle for the role of Superman through an intensive training program supervised by David Prowse, who played Darth Vader.
Margot Kidder performed many of her own stunts in Superman (1978), including the helicopter rescue scene.
The helicopter rescue scene in Superman (1978) is considered one of the most iconic moments in superhero cinema history.
"Easy, miss. I've got you." "You've got me? Who's got you?!" — The classic exchange between Superman and Lois Lane during the helicopter rescue.
Superman (1978) used the tagline 'You'll believe a man can fly' and pioneered visual effects techniques that made aerial superhero sequences convincing.
The Superman (1978) flying effects were achieved by Geoffrey Unsworth and Les Bowie using a combination of front projection and wire rigs.
Superman II (1980) featured the epic battle between Superman and the three Kryptonian villains — Zod, Ursa, and Non — in the streets of Metropolis.
The battle in Metropolis in Superman II is often cited as the template for superhero city battles in later films.
Sarah Douglas played Ursa in Superman II, delivering a memorably cold and ruthless performance as Zod's lieutenant.
Jack O'Halloran played Non, the mute but immensely powerful member of Zod's trio in Superman II.
The Niagara Falls sequence in Superman II, where Lois discovers Clark is Superman, differs significantly between the Donner Cut and the Lester version.
In the Richard Donner Cut of Superman II, Lois shoots Clark with a blank to prove he's Superman, rather than the clumsy fireplace reveal.
Superman III (1983) grossed $80 million worldwide, significantly less than its predecessors due to its comedic tone.
The 'evil Superman' sequence in Superman III, where a corrupted Superman straightens the Leaning Tower of Pisa, is one of the film's most memorable moments.
Annette O'Toole played Lana Lang in Superman III and later played Martha Kent in Smallville, making her one of the few actors to appear in both adaptations.
Superman IV's Nuclear Man villain was considered one of the weakest in the franchise and suffered from the film's extremely low budget.
Superman IV was the last film Christopher Reeve made as Superman, and despite its flaws, his performance remained committed.
Jon Cryer played Lex Luthor's nephew Lenny in Superman IV and later played Lex Luthor himself in the TV series Supergirl.
"Why do they need a hero? They need to look inside themselves and see the hero within." — This philosophy has guided many Superman writers.
Man of Steel (2013) showed the destruction of Metropolis during the battle with Zod's forces, with estimated fictional damage costs in the trillions.
The 'first flight' sequence in Man of Steel, where Clark takes off for the first time with Hans Zimmer's score soaring, is widely praised as one of the best scenes.
Kevin Costner played Jonathan Kent in Man of Steel, whose death in a tornado was one of the film's most controversial creative choices.
Diane Lane played Martha Kent in Man of Steel and its sequels, bringing gravitas to the role of Clark's Earth mother.
Laurence Fishburne became the first Black actor to play Perry White in Man of Steel.
Man of Steel's visual style, with its muted color palette and handheld camera work, was influenced by Terrence Malick's filmmaking.
"You just have to decide what kind of man you want to grow up to be, Clark. Whoever that man is, he's going to change the world." — Jonathan Kent in Man of Steel.
The Kryptonian language in Man of Steel was developed by linguist Christine Schreyer specifically for the film.
The codex in Man of Steel, containing the genetic information of all future Kryptonians, was bonded to baby Kal-El by Jor-El.
Superman's black suit, worn during his resurrection in Zack Snyder's Justice League, references the recovery suit from the Return of Superman comics.
"Tell me, do you bleed? You will." — Batman's threat to Superman in Batman v Superman became one of the film's most quoted lines.
The warehouse fight scene in Batman v Superman, where Batman rescues Martha Kent, is considered one of the best fight sequences in superhero cinema.
Batman v Superman's Ultimate Edition, running 183 minutes, was better received than the theatrical cut and restored important plot points.
Holly Hunter played Senator Finch in Batman v Superman, representing the government's attempt to hold Superman accountable.
The Superman statue in Batman v Superman, with 'FALSE GOD' spray-painted on it, symbolized the public's divided feelings about the Man of Steel.
Jeremy Irons played Alfred Pennyworth in Batman v Superman and Justice League, providing sardonic commentary on Batman's obsession with Superman.
"No one stays good in this world." — Superman's disillusioned words in Batman v Superman reflected the film's darker interpretation of the character.
Justice League (2017) went through extensive reshoots under Joss Whedon after Zack Snyder stepped away due to a family tragedy.
Henry Cavill's mustache for Mission: Impossible — Fallout had to be digitally removed for Justice League reshoots, creating a noticeable and mocked CGI upper lip.
Zack Snyder's Justice League (2021) was released on HBO Max after a massive fan campaign using the hashtag #ReleasetheSnyderCut.
The Snyder Cut movement is one of the most successful fan campaigns in entertainment history, leading to a $70 million completion of the director's vision.
In Zack Snyder's Justice League, Superman wears the black suit and is shown to be far more powerful than the combined might of the other League members.
"This is my world. You are my world." — Superman to Lois Lane in Zack Snyder's Justice League, choosing love over rage.
The Adventures of Superman TV series (1952-1958) was one of the first television shows to feature a superhero and helped establish the genre on TV.
Noel Neill played Lois Lane in the Superman serials and later seasons of Adventures of Superman, making her the longest-serving live-action Lois.
Phyllis Coates played Lois Lane in the first season of Adventures of Superman before being replaced by Noel Neill.
Jack Larson played Jimmy Olsen in Adventures of Superman and became closely identified with the role for the rest of his career.
Dean Cain, who played Superman in Lois & Clark, was a former professional football player before turning to acting.
Lois & Clark's wedding episode aired in 1996, and DC Comics timed the comic book wedding to coincide with the TV event.
"I'm Superman. I can do anything." — While various versions of Superman have echoed this sentiment, the character's stories often explore its limits.
Smallville's Allison Mack played Chloe Sullivan, an original character created for the show who was later introduced into the comics.
Kristin Kreuk played Lana Lang in Smallville, serving as Clark's primary love interest before Lois Lane's introduction.
Erica Durance joined Smallville in season 4 as Lois Lane and remained with the show through its finale.
John Schneider and Annette O'Toole played Jonathan and Martha Kent in Smallville, forming the emotional backbone of the series.
Smallville's season 4 featured a reinterpretation of the Kryptonian stones and their connection to the Fortress of Solitude.
The Smallville finale showed Tom Welling finally donning the Superman suit, though the full costume was largely shown through CGI.
"In the end, I just want Superman to be a good person." — Tom Welling on playing Clark Kent for ten years.
Superman & Lois explored themes of parenting, small-town America, and economic hardship alongside traditional superhero action.
Tyler Hoechlin first appeared as Superman in Supergirl before getting his own spinoff series Superman & Lois.
Superman & Lois was praised for its cinematic quality, with production values rivaling many feature films.
"The symbol of the House of El means stronger together." — A recurring theme in Superman & Lois emphasizing family unity.
Supergirl (2015-2021) starred Melissa Benoist as Kara Zor-El and featured Tyler Hoechlin as a recurring Superman.
Helen Slater played Supergirl in the 1984 film and later appeared as Clark's mother in Smallville.
Superman and the Mole-Men (1951) was the first Superman feature film and served as a pilot for the Adventures of Superman TV series.
The Superman musical It's a Bird... It's a Plane... It's Superman! opened on Broadway in 1966 but was not a commercial success.
Superman has been parodied countless times, from Seinfeld's constant references to SNL sketches to Shrek's fairy tale sendups.
Jerry Seinfeld is a famous Superman fan, and Superman references appear throughout Seinfeld, including a Superman magnet on Jerry's fridge.
"I'm also just a guy from Kansas." — Superman reminding people of his humble upbringing despite his godlike powers.
The Superman/Batman comic series explored the contrasting philosophies of the two heroes, with chapters alternating between their inner monologues.
Public Enemies, the first arc of Superman/Batman, pitted the duo against a Kryptonite asteroid and President Lex Luthor.
Lex Luthor was actually elected President of the United States in DC Comics, serving from 2000 to 2004 in publication terms.
"Some men aren't looking for anything logical. Some men just want to see Superman fail." — An observation about Lex Luthor's obsessive hatred.
Lex Luthor has worn a suit of Kryptonite-powered battle armor in many incarnations, allowing him to physically fight Superman.
In many continuities, Lex Luthor and Clark Kent were childhood friends in Smallville before becoming bitter enemies.
Lex Luthor's hatred of Superman often stems from jealousy — he cannot accept that an alien, not a human, is the most powerful being on Earth.
"Do you know how much power I'd have to give up to be President? The presidency is beneath me." — Lex Luthor demonstrating his astronomical ego.
Mercy Graves, Luthor's bodyguard and assistant, was created for Superman: The Animated Series before being introduced into comics.
Superman's relationship with the military has been complex — sometimes he's an ally, other times he's seen as a potential threat to national security.
General Sam Lane, Lois Lane's father, has frequently been depicted as distrustful of Superman and aliens in general.
Project Cadmus is a government-run genetics lab in DC Comics that has created clones and weapons designed to counter Superman.
The Clone Saga in Superman comics involved multiple attempts to clone Superman, most notably resulting in Superboy (Kon-El).
Match is a failed clone of Superboy who served as his evil counterpart, similar to Bizarro's relationship with Superman.
Superman's solar absorption means he is essentially a living solar battery, growing more powerful the longer he is exposed to yellow sunlight.
Some writers have depicted Superman as needing to eat and sleep to maintain his mental health, even if his body doesn't strictly require it.
Superman's super-speed allows him to operate at velocities approaching the speed of light in most modern continuities.
The Flash and Superman have raced multiple times in comics, with results varying — but the Flash is generally accepted as faster.
"Those races were for charity, Clark." — The Flash hinting to Superman that he was holding back during their famous races.
Superman can vibrate his molecules at super-speed to pass through solid objects, though this power is used less frequently than the Flash's phasing.
Superman's invulnerability has limits — sufficient force from beings like Darkseid or Doomsday can injure him.
Clark Kent's glasses disguise, often criticized as implausible, has been justified variously through super-hypnosis, subtle facial muscle control, and social psychology.
"It's a remarkable dichotomy. In many ways, Clark is more real than Superman. Clark is how he views humanity." — A common analysis of the dual identity.
The Daily Planet building with its iconic globe on top is one of the most recognized fictional landmarks in comics.
Cat Grant is a recurring Daily Planet character who has been depicted as everything from a gossip columnist to an editor.
Ron Troupe, Steve Lombard, and other Daily Planet staff have added depth to Superman's supporting cast over the years.
Superman's pal Bibbo Bibbowski is a tough but lovable bartender in Metropolis who idolizes Superman.
Professor Emil Hamilton is a scientist who has both helped and opposed Superman in various storylines.
"This looks like a job for Superman!" — Clark Kent's classic catchphrase before changing into his costume, originating from the radio show.
Superman originally did not have X-ray vision — this power was added in the early 1940s comics.
Superman's X-ray vision cannot see through lead, a weakness that Lex Luthor frequently exploits by lining his facilities with lead.
The Superman dynasty in DC One Million includes descendants who continue his legacy thousands of years into the future.
Val-Zod is an Earth-2 Superman of Kryptonian origin who took up the mantle in the New 52's Earth 2 series.
Calvin Ellis, an African American Superman based on Barack Obama, protects Earth-23 in the DC Multiverse.
The Multiverse concept allows for infinite versions of Superman, including Red Son, Kingdom Come, Injustice, and many others.
Superman of Earth-2 (Kal-L) was the original Golden Age Superman who married his Lois Lane and lived into old age.
Ultraman is the evil counterpart of Superman from Earth-3, a member of the Crime Syndicate who grows stronger from Kryptonite.
"Up, up, and away!" — Superman's classic exclamation when taking flight, first used in the 1940s radio serial.
The Silver Age of Comics (1956-1970) saw Superman's powers expand to absurd levels, including the ability to sneeze away solar systems.
Curt Swan was the definitive Superman artist from the 1960s through the 1980s, drawing the character for over two decades.
Wayne Boring was one of the earliest Superman artists, establishing the character's visual identity in the Golden Age.
Joe Shuster's early Superman art was rough but dynamic, establishing the visual template that all subsequent artists would build upon.
Jim Lee's artwork on Superman has been among the most popular, particularly his work on Superman Unchained (2013-2014).
"Peace on Earth" (1998) by Paul Dini and Alex Ross is a beautiful oversized one-shot showing Superman's attempt to end world hunger.
In Peace on Earth, Superman learns that even with all his power, some problems — like global hunger — can't be solved with strength alone.
"I once thought I could protect the world by myself, but I was wrong. Working together, we saved the planet." — Superman on the value of teamwork.
Superman has lifted objects ranging from buildings to tectonic plates to entire planets depending on the era and writer.
In All-Star Superman, Superman bench-presses the weight of the Earth with one arm while answering questions from scientists.
Superman once held a miniature black hole in his hands, preventing it from destroying Earth.
The concept of 'Superman Blue' and 'Superman Red' split the character into two energy-based beings in the late 1990s.
The electric blue Superman costume of 1997-1998 was one of the most controversial redesigns in the character's history.
Superman's traditional red and blue color scheme has remained fundamentally unchanged since 1938, making it one of the most enduring designs in pop culture.
"In a world of ordinary mortals, you are a superman." — the phrasing echoes Nietzsche's Übermensch, though Superman's creators subverted that concept entirely.
Friedrich Nietzsche's concept of the Übermensch (Superman) influenced the name, but Siegel and Shuster's character embodies humility and compassion rather than dominance.
The word 'Superman' entered the English language partly thanks to George Bernard Shaw's play Man and Superman (1903), predating the comic character.
Max Landis wrote a popular short film called 'The Death and Return of Superman' (2012) explaining the comic storyline's cultural significance.
Superman: Doomsday (2007) was the first DC Universe Animated Original Movie, adapting the Death of Superman storyline.
The Death of Superman (2018) and Reign of the Supermen (2019) were animated films that provided a more faithful adaptation of the comic storyline.
Superman vs. The Elite (2012) adapted the acclaimed 'What's So Funny About Truth, Justice & the American Way?' storyline.
All-Star Superman (2011) was an animated film adaptation of the Grant Morrison/Frank Quitely comic, featuring James Denton as Superman.
Superman: Red Son (2020) was adapted into an animated film that explored the Soviet Superman concept.
Superman: Unbound (2013) adapted the Brainiac storyline by Geoff Johns and Gary Frank.
The 'World of Cardboard' speech from Justice League Unlimited is considered one of the greatest Superman moments in animation history.
"What we can do for these people, we must do. That's what Superman means." — A sentiment expressed across multiple Superman adaptations.
Superman has inspired real-world heroism — studies have shown that exposure to Superman imagery can increase people's willingness to help others.
The Superman Foundation and various charitable organizations have used the character to promote literacy, health awareness, and social causes.
Christopher Reeve's Superman performance is frequently ranked as the greatest superhero portrayal in film history in critics' polls.
"Somewhere in our darkest night, we made up the story of a man who will never let us down." — Grant Morrison on why Superman endures.
Superman: Brainiac (2008) by Geoff Johns and Gary Frank is one of the most acclaimed modern Superman stories, redefining Brainiac as a genuine threat.
In Superman: Brainiac, Brainiac kills Jonathan Kent, marking one of the most emotionally devastating moments in modern Superman comics.
Superman: Last Son (2006-2008) by Geoff Johns and Richard Donner (yes, the film director) introduced a Kryptonian child who crash-lands on Earth.
Geoff Johns and Richard Donner's collaboration on Action Comics brought the filmmaker's cinematic sensibility to the comic books.
The New 52 reboot in 2011 reimagined Superman as younger and more aggressive, with a redesigned armor-like costume and no red trunks.
DC Rebirth in 2016 restored the pre-New 52 married Superman with his son Jon, returning to a more classic interpretation.
Superman's cape is often used symbolically — it has served as a blanket for rescued children, a stretcher for the wounded, and a shroud for the fallen.
"The 'S' stands for hope, and that's what Superman provides. He gives us hope that we can be better." — A common sentiment among Superman writers and fans.
Superman has been translated into virtually every language and published in countries around the world, making him a truly global icon.
In Japan, Superman inspired the creation of many characters, and the superhero concept he established influenced the development of sentai and henshin hero genres.
Superman's influence on popular culture is so pervasive that the word 'superman' has become a common English word meaning an exceptionally powerful or accomplished person.
"Faster than a speeding bullet, more powerful than a locomotive, able to leap tall buildings in a single bound" — the iconic radio introduction defined Superman's powers for generations.
Bud Collyer was the first voice actor to play Superman, starring in the radio serial from 1940 to 1951 and the Filmation animated series.
Collyer developed the technique of lowering his voice when Clark Kent became Superman, establishing a vocal tradition followed by later actors.
Kirk Alyn's Superman serial (1948) was one of the most successful movie serials ever produced.
The 1950s Adventures of Superman show avoided excessive violence, instead showing Superman solving crimes and catching criminals rather than punching them.
"Great Scott!" — Superman's classic exclamation, used across numerous adaptations from comics to film.
Lois Lane's solo comic series, Superman's Girl Friend, Lois Lane, ran from 1958 to 1974 and was one of DC's best-selling titles.
Lois Lane has been depicted as one of the bravest characters in the DC Universe — she regularly puts herself in danger to get the story.
"I don't need you to save me. I need you to trust me." — Lois Lane asserting her independence, a theme across many adaptations.
Clark Kent proposed to Lois Lane in Superman #662 (1991), and their wedding in 1996 was a major comics event.
In the New 52 continuity, Superman dated Wonder Woman, a controversial change from his traditional relationship with Lois Lane.
Superman's Fortress of Solitude has been located at the North Pole, the South Pole, the Amazon rainforest, and even inside a mountain, depending on the continuity.
The Fortress of Solitude typically contains a memorial to Krypton, alien zoo specimens, the bottled city of Kandor, and various trophies from Superman's adventures.
Superman has a key to the Fortress of Solitude that weighs millions of tons, ensuring no one without super-strength can open the door.
"Have you ever tried to see the world through his eyes? He doesn't just see danger — he sees everything. Every cry for help. And he answers them all." — Lois Lane describing Superman's burden.
Superman's vulnerability to Kryptonite has been used as a metaphor for the idea that everyone, no matter how powerful, has a weakness.
Pink Kryptonite, in a non-canon story, caused Superman to develop an attraction to Jimmy Olsen, in a rare moment of comic book humor about the substance.
White Kryptonite kills all plant life and has been used against plant-based villains in Superman comics.
Jewel Kryptonite enhances the psychic powers of Phantom Zone villains.
Platinum Kryptonite can grant a human Kryptonian-level powers permanently, as seen when Batman was offered it.
"A man can't just sit around" — Jonathan Kent's practical Kansas wisdom that shaped Clark's work ethic.
The Superman/Shazam dynamic explores two different approaches to power — Superman earned trust over years while Billy Batson was given godlike power as a child.
Captain Marvel (now called Shazam) and Superman have fought several times, with the magical nature of Shazam's powers making him one of the few beings who can truly challenge Superman.
Superman once fought Goku in a popular web series called Death Battle, sparking intense debate among fans of both franchises. Superman won both matches.
"What makes Superman a hero is not that he has power, but that he has the wisdom and the maturity to use the power wisely." — Christopher Reeve.
Action Comics reached issue #1000 in April 2018, making Superman's debut title the first American comic book to reach that milestone.
Action Comics #1000 featured stories by multiple creators and restored Superman's red trunks to his costume.
Superman #1, a solo book separate from Action Comics, was published in 1939 and reprinted the earliest Superman stories.
The Superman comic strip ran in newspapers from 1939 to 1966, reaching millions of readers in daily and Sunday editions.
Superman co-creator Joe Shuster secretly drew fetish illustrations under a pseudonym to make ends meet after losing the Superman rights.
The Siegel and Shuster story is often cited as a cautionary tale about creators' rights in the comic book industry.
In 2013, a court ruling allowed DC Comics to retain full rights to Superman, ending decades of legal challenges by the Siegel and Shuster estates.
"Worst of all, we signed away our creation for nothing. They took our child." — Jerry Siegel on selling the Superman rights for $130.
Neal Adams was instrumental in campaigning for Siegel and Shuster's recognition and financial compensation in the 1970s.
Superman's influence on comic books was so profound that the Golden Age of Comics (1938-1956) is dated from his first appearance.
Within a year of Superman's debut, dozens of imitators appeared, including Wonder Man (sued out of existence by DC) and Captain Marvel.
The lawsuit DC Comics filed against Fawcett Comics over Captain Marvel (now Shazam) being too similar to Superman lasted from 1941 to 1953.
By 1940, Superman comics were selling over 1.25 million copies per issue, making him the most popular character in America.
"I believe in second chances. I believe in redemption. But, more than anything, I believe in my friends." — Superman emphasizing his core values.
General Zod's origin as a well-intentioned extremist who genuinely believes he's protecting Krypton makes him one of Superman's most compelling villains.
Faora is a Kryptonian villain often associated with General Zod, depicted as a deadly martial artist in Man of Steel.
Antje Traue's portrayal of Faora-Ul in Man of Steel was widely praised, with her fight scene against the military being a standout action sequence.
The World Engine in Man of Steel was Zod's device to terraform Earth into a new Krypton, threatening all human life.
"I will find him!" — General Zod's threatening message broadcast to Earth in Man of Steel, demanding Kal-El's surrender.
Doomsday's origin varies by continuity, but he is typically depicted as a prehistoric Kryptonian experiment designed to be the ultimate killing machine.
Doomsday is one of the few villains to have actually killed Superman, making him uniquely significant in DC lore.
The famous fight between Superman and Doomsday leveled much of Metropolis and ended with both combatants delivering fatal blows to each other.
"This is the story of a man who could fly. Of all the stories to tell about Superman, this one is important because it's the last one." — Grant Morrison's All-Star Superman narration.
In All-Star Superman, Superman creates a miniature sun to sustain Earth after he sacrifices himself to repair the real sun.
All-Star Superman's Twelve Labors structure mirrors the Twelve Labors of Hercules, connecting Superman to classical mythology.
"Doomed planet. Desperate scientists. Last hope. Kindly couple." — Grant Morrison's four-panel distillation of Superman's origin in All-Star Superman.
Kingdom Come's Superman retired to his farm after a new generation of violent heroes made his methods seem outdated.
In Kingdom Come, Superman is eventually drawn back into action when the new heroes' recklessness threatens to destroy the world.
Norman McCay, a minister, serves as the reader's POV character in Kingdom Come, guided by the Spectre to witness Superman's return.
Alex Ross based Kingdom Come's older Superman partly on actor Gregory Peck, giving him a distinguished, weighty presence.
"When you can do the things that I can, but you don't, and then the bad things happen — they happen because of you." — This Spider-Man quote echoes an identical philosophy Superman has long embodied.
Superman's first encounter with Batman in comics was in Superman #76 (1952), where they accidentally discovered each other's identities while sharing a cabin on a ship.
The World's Finest Comics series ran from 1941 to 1986, teaming Superman and Batman in every issue from #71 onward.
Frank Miller's The Dark Knight Returns (1986) established the modern rivalry/friendship dynamic between Superman and Batman.
In The Dark Knight Returns, Superman works as a government agent and is sent to stop Batman, leading to their legendary fight.
"I want you to remember, Clark. In all the years to come, in your most private moments, I want you to remember my hand at your throat." — Batman to Superman in The Dark Knight Returns.
Superman has appeared in every major DC Comics crossover event, from Crisis on Infinite Earths to Infinite Crisis to Final Crisis.
In Final Crisis (2008), Superman sings at a frequency that shatters Darkseid's essence, defeating the villain through the power of music.
Superman Beyond, a tie-in to Final Crisis, shows Superman traveling through the multiverse and encountering the concept of story itself.
"To be continued" — the literal last words written by Superman in the Book of Infinite Pages in Final Crisis: Superman Beyond.
Infinite Crisis (2005-2006) featured the return of Earth-Two Superman, who had become disillusioned with the main DC Universe.
Superman lost his powers entirely for a year following Infinite Crisis, spending that time as an ordinary Clark Kent.
The 'Grounded' storyline (2010-2011) had Superman walk across America to reconnect with ordinary people, though it divided fans on execution.
H'El on Earth (2012-2013) introduced a Kryptonian villain named H'El who wanted to travel back in time to save Krypton.
The Warworld Saga (2021-2022) by Phillip Kennedy Johnson saw Superman enslaved on Mongul's Warworld and leading a revolution.
"Hope is not a feeling. Hope is a choice you make." — A modern Superman sentiment emphasizing agency over passivity.
Superman's heat vision can be adjusted from pinpoint precision to wide-beam devastation, and he has used it for everything from welding to lobotomizing Darkseid's brain.
In some continuities, Superman can use microscopic vision to see individual atoms and subatomic particles.
Superman has been depicted as capable of processing information at superhuman speeds, effectively giving him a degree of super-intelligence.
"Do good to others and every man can be a Superman." — A quote capturing the democratic ideal at the heart of the character.
Tom Taylor's Superman: Son of Kal-El series explored Jon Kent taking over the Superman mantle while dealing with contemporary social issues.
Joshua Williamson's Superman run (2023-present) has focused on the Lex Luthor/Superman dynamic and introduced Marilyn Moonlight and other new elements.
Action Comics under Phillip Kennedy Johnson explored Superman's Kryptonian heritage more deeply than many previous runs.
Brian Michael Bendis wrote Superman and Action Comics from 2018 to 2020, aging up Jon Kent and revealing Clark's identity to the world.
The decision to have Superman publicly reveal his identity as Clark Kent in 2019 was one of the biggest status quo changes in the character's history.
Superman's rogues gallery, while not as celebrated as Batman's, includes formidable threats like Brainiac, Darkseid, Lex Luthor, and Doomsday.
Mister Miracle and Big Barda, New Gods from Apokolips, have been frequent Superman allies in their shared fight against Darkseid.
The Fourth World, Jack Kirby's epic cosmic mythology featuring Apokolips and New Genesis, intersects deeply with Superman's stories.
Jack Kirby's Jimmy Olsen run in the 1970s introduced concepts like the DNA Project and Intergang that became Superman staples.
"There's always a way." — Superman's refusal to accept defeat, a mantra that defines his approach to every challenge.
Superman's solar flare power, introduced in 2015, allows him to expel all stored solar energy in a massive explosion, temporarily leaving him powerless.
The Superman Revenge Squad is a team of villains united by their hatred of Superman, including various combinations of his rogues.
Titano the Super-Ape is a giant Kryptonite-vision-shooting gorilla and one of Superman's more unusual recurring foes.
The Prankster is a Superman villain who uses elaborate practical jokes and schemes to commit crimes.
Terra-Man is an old-west-themed Superman villain who was abducted by aliens as a child and given extraterrestrial weaponry.
"Clark Kent is Superman's critique on the whole human race." — This controversial quote from Kill Bill Vol. 2 sparked widespread debate about Superman's nature.
Many Superman scholars disagree with the Kill Bill interpretation, arguing that Clark Kent is Superman's true self, shaped by the Kents' values.
Superman's ability to inspire other heroes is his greatest power — characters from Steel to Superboy to the entire Legion of Super-Heroes exist because of his example.
"The world doesn't need a savior. But every day I hear people crying for one." — Superman acknowledging the paradox of his existence.
Superman and Lex Luthor's relationship is often described as the fundamental conflict between power used for others versus power used for self.
In some stories, Lex Luthor has found a cure for cancer but refuses to share it because he's too obsessed with defeating Superman.
"I could have made everyone see. I could have saved the world if it wasn't for you." — Lex Luthor blaming Superman for his own failures.
Superman's connection to Smallville grounds him — returning to the Kent farm reminds him of the values Jonathan and Martha taught him.
"It's never as bad as it seems. You're stronger than you think you are." — Superman offering reassurance, as he often does.
Kelex is a Kryptonian service robot that maintains the Fortress of Solitude and provides Superman with information about Kryptonian culture.
The Eradicator device was originally intended to preserve Kryptonian culture at any cost, making it a fundamentalist threat to Earth's diversity.
Superman's cape is traditionally depicted as indestructible or at least extremely durable, made from the blankets that wrapped baby Kal-El.
Martha Kent sewed Clark's first Superman costume from the indestructible Kryptonian blankets in most origin stories.
"It doesn't matter where you were born, or what you look like, or what planet you come from. As long as you're a good person." — Superman championing acceptance.
Superman once arm-wrestled Hercules in a comic and noted the demigod was actually quite strong.
Vartox is a Superman ally based on Sean Connery's appearance in the film Zardoz, and is one of the more outlandish characters in Superman lore.
Superman's birthday is traditionally celebrated on February 29 (Leap Day) in the comics, making him an 'impossible' birthday.
"The American Way" portion of Superman's motto has been debated for decades — some see it as patriotic, others as limiting for a global hero.
Superman renounced his American citizenship in Action Comics #900 (2011), declaring he was tired of his actions being seen as U.S. policy. The story was controversial.
Despite that story, subsequent writers have generally ignored the renunciation and Superman continues to be associated with American ideals.
Superman's DNA has been studied, cloned, and weaponized by virtually every villain and government agency in the DC Universe at some point.
Cadmus Labs created multiple Superman clones, most notably Superboy (Kon-El) and the monster called Match.
"Why am I so different from them?" — Young Clark Kent questioning his alienness, a scene that resonates with anyone who has ever felt like an outsider.
Superman has been depicted playing various sports — in one famous cover, he played baseball against Batman's team.
In All-Star Superman #10, Clark Kent interviews Lex Luthor on death row, and Luthor reveals his admiration for Superman without knowing Clark is Superman.
Superman has had crossovers with He-Man, the Thundercats, Bugs Bunny, and even the Terminator in special comics.
The Superman/Aliens crossover sent Superman to a world with a red sun where he lost his powers and had to survive against Xenomorphs.
Superman has been featured on United States postage stamps multiple times, cementing his status as an American cultural icon.
The Superman shield is one of the most recognized symbols in the world, comparable to religious and national symbols in its ubiquity.
"Nobody can be exactly like me. But then, I've always encouraged people to be themselves." — Superman embracing individuality.
Superman's Arctic Fortress has sometimes been depicted with a giant golden key kept under the doormat — visible to all but immovable by anyone without super-strength.
The bottled city of Kandor was eventually enlarged in some storylines, creating New Krypton and bringing thousands of Kryptonians to Earth's solar system.
The New Krypton storyline (2008-2010) explored what happens when 100,000 Kryptonians suddenly appear on Earth, each with Superman's powers.
"Men are still good. We fight, we kill, we betray one another, but we can rebuild. We can do better. We will. We have to." — Batman's eulogy for Superman in Batman v Superman.
Superman's heat vision has been depicted in various colors — red, blue, and orange — depending on the artist and adaptation.
In the animated series, Superman's eyes glow red before firing heat vision, creating an iconic visual cue used in later adaptations.
Superman and Darkseid's final confrontation in Justice League Unlimited saw Superman unleash his full power, delivering one of animation's greatest fight scenes.
"I feel like I live in a world made of cardboard." — Superman's admission before the Darkseid fight perfectly captures the restraint he shows daily.
Darkseid's Omega Beams can lock onto a target and follow them unerringly, but Superman has dodged them on multiple occasions.
The Anti-Life Equation, Darkseid's ultimate weapon, strips free will from sentient beings and represents the philosophical opposite of everything Superman stands for.
Superman and the Authority (2021) by Grant Morrison paired Superman with a team of antiheroes when his powers were fading.
Action Comics #1 was printed with a run of about 200,000 copies. Only about 100 copies are estimated to survive today.
A copy of Action Comics #1 in near-mint condition sold for $3.25 million in 2014 and another for $6.16 million in 2024.
The Library of Congress inducted Superman into its collection as a culturally significant American icon.
"He's the embodiment of human aspiration — an alien who became the most human of us all." — A common analysis of Superman's paradoxical character.
Christopher Reeve lobbied hard to include scenes of Clark Kent at the Kent farm in Superman (1978), believing they were essential to the character.
The Smallville scenes in Superman (1978), featuring Glenn Ford as Jonathan Kent, are some of the most emotionally powerful in the film.
Glenn Ford's performance as Jonathan Kent, particularly his death scene, was lauded for its quiet dignity and emotional truth.
"All those things I can do. All those powers. And I couldn't even save him." — Christopher Reeve's Clark Kent reflecting on Jonathan Kent's death.
Jeff East played young Clark Kent in Superman (1978), though Christopher Reeve's voice was dubbed over his performance.
Marc McClure played Jimmy Olsen in all four Christopher Reeve Superman films and in Supergirl (1984).
"Statistically speaking, it's still the safest way to travel." — Superman to Lois Lane after saving her on the airplane, showcasing his dry wit.
The 'Can You Read My Mind' sequence in Superman (1978) features Margot Kidder's internal monologue as Superman flies her over Metropolis.
Superman (1978) was filmed largely at Pinewood Studios in England, with location shooting in New York, Canada, and New Mexico.
The planet Krypton scenes in Superman (1978) used crystalline sets designed by John Barry, creating an otherworldly aesthetic.
Gene Hackman refused to shave his head for Lex Luthor, so bald caps and wigs were used instead.
Ned Beatty played Otis, Lex Luthor's bumbling henchman, in Superman (1978), providing comic relief.
Valerie Perrine played Miss Teschmacher, Lex Luthor's girlfriend in Superman (1978), who ultimately helps Superman out of compassion.
"It's amazing that brain can generate enough power to keep those legs moving." — Lex Luthor about Otis in Superman (1978), showcasing Gene Hackman's dry delivery.
Superman (1978) revolutionized the superhero genre and proved that comic book characters could be taken seriously on the big screen.
Without the success of Superman (1978), the modern superhero film genre — from Batman (1989) to the MCU — might never have developed.
"He'll be different from all the rest. But he'll be a force for good." — Martha Kent about raising Clark in many adaptations echoes the hope at Superman's core.
David Corenswet, who plays Superman in the 2025 James Gunn film, stands 6'4" — taller than any previous live-action Superman actor.
James Gunn's Superman (2025) features Krypto the Superdog as a significant character, the first live-action film to do so.
Rachel Brosnahan, known for The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel, was cast as Lois Lane in James Gunn's Superman (2025).
James Gunn wrote the screenplay for his Superman film, aiming to capture the character's inherent goodness and hopefulness.
Edi Gathegi was cast as Mister Terrific in James Gunn's Superman (2025), introducing a new DC Universe supporting character.
Isabela Merced was cast as Hawkgirl in James Gunn's Superman (2025), expanding the DCU roster beyond the Man of Steel.
Nathan Fillion was cast as Guy Gardner/Green Lantern in James Gunn's Superman (2025).
Superman (2025) marks the beginning of James Gunn and Peter Safran's new DC Universe, distinct from the previous DCEU.
"Superman is about kindness. It's about being the best version of yourself." — James Gunn on his approach to the 2025 film.
The first trailer for James Gunn's Superman broke viewership records, indicating massive audience anticipation for the new interpretation.
Superman has appeared in more live-action films than any other DC superhero character.
The total worldwide box office of all Superman films exceeds $2 billion.
Superman Returns used the same crystal-based Kryptonian aesthetic as the Christopher Reeve films to maintain visual continuity.
Brandon Routh later returned to the Superman role in the Arrowverse Crisis on Infinite Earths crossover, playing the Kingdom Come version.
The Arrowverse Crisis on Infinite Earths featured multiple Superman actors — Tom Welling, Brandon Routh, and Tyler Hoechlin — sharing the screen.

"Superman is not brave. He's smart, he's handsome, but he's not brave. He has nothing to fear. When facing danger, he's not brave, he's just playing along." — A debatable take attributed to various writers.
Many writers have countered that perspective by noting Superman's bravery lies in emotional vulnerability — choosing to care in a world that could hurt him.
Superman's greatest battle may not be against any villain but against despair — the temptation to give up on a world that doesn't always deserve saving.
"It's never easy. The cape doesn't make it easy. But if I can make even one person's day better, it's worth it." — A sentiment Superman has expressed across many stories.
Superman has been depicted as vegetarian or at least preferring simple farm food in several modern interpretations.
Clark Kent's favorite food is often depicted as beef bourguignon or his mother's apple pie, depending on the writer.
Superman once worked as a television news anchor in the comics during the 1970s, reflecting the changing media landscape.
Morgan Edge, a media mogul, has been both a Superman ally and antagonist across different continuities.
The Daily Star was the original name of the newspaper where Clark Kent worked before it was changed to the Daily Planet.
"Headlines don't sell papers. Newsboys sell papers." — Perry White's old-school journalism philosophy.
Superman's impact on the comic book industry cannot be overstated — he literally created the superhero genre and saved the comics medium during the late 1930s.
Before Superman, comic books were primarily reprint collections of newspaper strips. His success created the original content comic book market.
National Allied Publications, which published Action Comics #1, eventually became DC Comics, with the 'DC' standing for Detective Comics.
Superman and Batman, DC's two flagship characters, have been in continuous publication since 1938 and 1939 respectively.
"Courage isn't the absence of fear. It's having the power to destroy everything and choosing not to." — A reflection on what makes Superman truly heroic.
